title: Shut It Down 1

jokes.text: It's for parking, Alex. / And emergencies, hence the name, emer... / You know, there's a logic to that.
jokes.setup: Tony explains why he didn't use the emergency brake during his out-of-control cab situation
jokes.punchline: Tony's literal interpretation that emergency brake is only for parking, Alex's interrupted explanation, then his resigned acceptance of Tony's logic
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Tony's literal mindset and Alex's exasperated reasonableness. The interrupted 'emer...' and resigned acceptance create perfect comedic timing.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: The emergency brake, it's for parking! You put the car in park, you pull the emergency brake. It's not for driving emergencies!

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Tony, the emergency brake is—

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: It's right there in the name, Alex. 'Emergency brake.' Not 'emergency while you're driving brake.' Emergency. Brake. Two separate things.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: That's not... you know what, never mind.

jokes.text: For a minute there I thought I forgot to close the old porthole.
jokes.setup: Drivers are complaining about cab safety issues
jokes.punchline: Louie's complete obliviousness to their concerns, worrying about his cage instead
jokes.explanation: Solid character beat showing Louie's self-centeredness, but relatively mild impact.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Safety issues? What safety issues? You guys are always complaining. You know what I'm worried about? My cage. Somebody's gotta protect me from you animals out there.

jokes.text: I thought most of these people were just about as ugly as they could get.
jokes.setup: Someone warns this could turn into an ugly mob
jokes.punchline: Louie's cruel observation about their physical appearance
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie insult, well-delivered but not particularly clever or original.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: An ugly mob? These guys ARE an ugly mob.

jokes.text: I knew I shouldn't have said that.
jokes.setup: Louie's immediate cruel response to the 'ugly mob' comment
jokes.punchline: Driver's immediate regret at giving Louie the opening
jokes.explanation: Nice quick beat showing self-awareness about setting up Louie's insult.

dialogue.speaker: Unknown driver
dialogue.line: You're an ugly mob.

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? Well, at least I'm not a two-bit hack who couldn't get a real job, so I gotta drive a cab for a living!

jokes.text: I'm going to write out a report. / Aw, come on! That never works! / Don't try to talk me out of it!
jokes.setup: Drivers demand action about dangerous cab conditions
jokes.punchline: Louie's hilariously inadequate bureaucratic response and mock determination
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie moment - bureaucratic non-solution delivered with mock heroism. The 'don't talk me out of it' line is particularly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Alright, alright, I hear you. You want safer cabs? Fine. I'm gonna take action. Real action.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Starting tomorrow, I'm gonna personally inspect every single cab in this garage.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I'm gonna check the brakes, the tires, the wipers... I'm gonna make sure every vehicle is in tip-top shape.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm even gonna wash 'em. How's that?

jokes.text: I am going to issue 'drive defensively' stickers.
jokes.setup: Louie admits a report isn't enough
jokes.punchline: Even more useless 'solution' than the report
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation of bureaucratic uselessness. The absurdity of stickers for broken brakes is perfectly Louie.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: A report? A report isn't enough!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I need action. I need results. I need something that'll actually solve this problem.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know what? Forget the report. Just give me a memo instead.

jokes.text: Go blow-dry your face.
jokes.setup: Drivers are blocking him from his cage
jokes.punchline: Nonsensical insult that makes no literal sense
jokes.explanation: Weird, memorable Louie-ism. Doesn't make logical sense but fits his character perfectly.

jokes.text: Not by the hair of my chinny-chin-chin.
jokes.setup: Drivers threaten to break down his cage door
jokes.punchline: Louie quotes the Three Little Pigs fairy tale
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd reference that perfectly fits the cage/house parallel. Shows Louie's weird literary knowledge.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You guys can huff and puff all you want, but you're not blowin' down this door. I built it outta bricks.

jokes.text: Hah-hah! Hah-hah!
jokes.setup: Louie emerges from secret passage
jokes.punchline: Triumphant villain laugh after his escape
jokes.explanation: Classic cartoon villain moment. Physical comedy beat with Louie's gleeful escape.

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: Mwahahaha!

jokes.text: unless, of course, Nardo wants to beat them out of me.
jokes.setup: Louie won't reveal his other secrets
jokes.punchline: Inappropriate sexual suggestion about Elaine
jokes.explanation: Typical Louie inappropriate comment, in character but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: No, Jim Petty is the backup goalie for the Rangers.
jokes.setup: Drivers trying to figure out who their shop steward is, suggesting Jim Petty
jokes.punchline: Complete non-sequitur about hockey
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist tangent showing how disorganized the drivers are. Random sports knowledge is funny in context.

dialogue.speaker: Driver
dialogue.line: Wait, you're saying Jim Petty is our shop steward? That doesn't make any sense. He doesn't even know what a union is!

dialogue.speaker: Driver 2
dialogue.line: Well, somebody's gotta do it.

dialogue.speaker: Driver
dialogue.line: Yeah, but Jim? The guy can't even remember his own phone number. Last week he thought he was playing hockey. He showed up to the garage with roller skates and a stick.

jokes.text: Just what I was afraid of... They're getting organized.
jokes.setup: Drivers have spent several minutes proving they have no idea who their shop steward is
jokes.punchline: Louie's sarcastic fear of their 'organization'
jokes.explanation: Perfect timing and irony. The contrast between their chaos and his 'fear' is expertly executed.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Oh, I'm terrified. I'm absolutely terrified of your organization. You know what scares me? The fact that you guys don't even know who your shop steward is. That's what keeps me up at night.

jokes.text: His name's Jack something or other. / Why? You want to nominate him? / No, I just couldn't think of his name.
jokes.setup: Driver brings up a red-headed guy who works weekends
jokes.punchline: Reveals he has no actual reason for mentioning him
jokes.explanation: Nice bit of everyday absurdity - bringing up someone for no reason except mental itch.

dialogue.speaker: Tony Banta
dialogue.line: You know that red-headed guy who works weekends? The one with the curly hair?

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: Yeah, what about him?

dialogue.speaker: Tony Banta
dialogue.line: Nothing. I just wanted to see if you knew who I was talking about.

jokes.text: Stand back and watch my dust.
jokes.setup: Tony elected shop steward, confident he'll get things done
jokes.punchline: Overconfident bravado that will obviously backfire
jokes.explanation: Sets up Tony's inevitable failure, but the line itself isn't particularly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I got elected shop steward. I'm gonna get this place running like clockwork. Better wages, better hours, better working conditions. I'm gonna walk in there and demand respect.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Tony, you're gonna get clobbered.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Louie, don't worry about me. I know how to handle these corporate types. I'm gonna be reasonable, but firm. They're gonna listen to me because I got the whole garage behind me.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Yeah, Tony. We're right behind you. About three blocks behind you.

jokes.text: Right foot, left foot, right foot...
jokes.setup: Louie invites Tony into his cage and Tony is clearly nervous
jokes.punchline: Tony literally talks himself through walking
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tony moment showing his anxiety and simple nature. Physical comedy through dialogue.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Okay, okay, one foot in front of the other. That's it. Left foot. Now the right foot. You can do this, Tony. Just keep moving. Left foot. Right foot. Left foot. Right foot.

jokes.text: And now that you're the shop steward, I'm sure you're going to want to spend time down there yourself.
jokes.setup: Louie describing the fancy executive restaurant
jokes.punchline: Smoothly implying Tony now belongs with 'executives'
jokes.explanation: Skillful manipulation showing Louie's cunning. The class appeal is perfectly calibrated to Tony.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know, Tony, this is a very fancy executive restaurant. The kind of place where important businessmen eat.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I think you belong here now.

jokes.text: I saw Jersey Joe Walcott in there the other night.
jokes.setup: Louie building up the restaurant's prestige
jokes.punchline: Name-drops an aging boxer to impress Tony
jokes.explanation: Good character beat showing Louie knows exactly what will impress Tony - sports celebrity.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know who was in here last night? Muhammad Ali's sparring partner from 1974.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah, he's been in a coma ever since.

jokes.text: It's called the monsoon. It's days before your sky is clear.
jokes.setup: Louie describing a special drink at the restaurant
jokes.punchline: Poetic description that sounds like bad advertising copy
jokes.explanation: Mildly clever wordplay that sounds like cheesy bar marketing. Fits Louie's pretentious side.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: We got a new drink at the restaurant. It's called 'The Sunrise.' You take vodka, orange juice, and a splash of grenadine. It's beautiful—the colors blend together like a painting. The way the light hits the glass, it's almost poetic.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Of course, the guy who invented it was drunk and threw up in the sink, but that's not the point.

jokes.text: I blew it, huh? / Big.
jokes.setup: Tony returns from being completely manipulated by Louie
jokes.punchline: Alex's blunt confirmation of Tony's spectacular failure
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy with 'Big.' Alex's deadpan delivery makes Tony's failure even funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Alex, I just realized something. Louie completely played me. I walked right into it.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Yeah.

jokes.text: I nominate Tony. / Jim! Why would you want to nominate Tony?! / There's no substitute for experience.
jokes.setup: They need a new shop steward after Tony's failure
jokes.punchline: Jim's backwards logic about Tony's 'experience' in failure
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist logic. Jim's sincere delivery of completely backwards reasoning is perfectly executed.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: We need a new shop steward after Tony's failure.

dialogue.speaker: Driver
dialogue.line: But Tony has experience.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: That's exactly the problem—his experience is in failing!

jokes.text: Well, see you tomorrow.
jokes.setup: Everyone is anxiously waiting for Elaine's crucial negotiation results
jokes.punchline: Jim casually leaves during the most important moment
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly timed character moment. Jim's oblivious departure during peak tension is perfectly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I'm gonna grab a coffee. Anyone want anything?

dialogue.speaker: Others
dialogue.line: Jim! Where are you going? Elaine's about to tell us how the negotiation went!

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I'll be right back. This will just take a minute.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: (returning an hour later) So what did I miss?

jokes.text: Oh, no, no, no! Oh, no, not a strike! Oh, fie!
jokes.setup: Drivers declare they're on strike
jokes.punchline: Louie's theatrical, obviously fake distress topped with archaic 'fie!'
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Louie's theatrical fakeness. The archaic 'fie!' is a perfect touch.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: A strike?! Fie! This is a disaster! A catastrophe! My beautiful garage! My precious cabs! How will I survive without squeezing every last penny out of you people?!

jokes.text: Daily News! Daily News! Hey, kid! You want to make some big bucks?
jokes.setup: Louie claims he can't find qualified replacement drivers
jokes.punchline: Immediately recruits a newspaper boy off the street
jokes.explanation: Perfect visual comedy and escalation. Shows exactly how unqualified his 'replacements' are.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I can't find qualified replacement drivers anywhere!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Hey kid! You with the papers! You wanna drive a cab?

dialogue.speaker: Newspaper Boy
dialogue.line: Sure, mister!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You're hired!

jokes.text: And remember... The big pedal's the brake.
jokes.setup: Louie is training completely unqualified replacement drivers
jokes.punchline: Ridiculously basic driving instruction for taxi drivers
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark comedy. The absurdly basic instruction highlights how dangerous this situation is.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Alright, listen up. First thing you gotta know about drivin' a cab: this is the steering wheel. You turn it left, you go left. You turn it right, you go right.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: These are the pedals. The one on the right makes you go faster. The one on the left makes you stop. Don't mix 'em up or you're gonna have a bad time.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: And this here is the meter. Every time it clicks, the customer pays more money. That's the best part of the job.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Now, when somebody waves at you from the street, that means they want a ride. Pick 'em up. That's how you make money. Simple.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Any questions? No? Good. You start tomorrow. Try not to crash into anything.

jokes.text: remember, red means 'stop,' green means 'go.'
jokes.setup: Instructing another replacement driver
jokes.punchline: Most basic traffic knowledge treated as advanced instruction
jokes.explanation: Escalates the theme of unqualified drivers, good callback structure.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Alright, listen up. I'm gonna teach you the fundamentals of driving in this city. First rule: when the light is green, you go. When it's red, you stop.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: When it's yellow, that means hurry up and go through before it turns red.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: One-way streets? You go one way. Not the other way.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: The big metal thing in front of the car? That's the hood. The big metal thing in back? That's the trunk. Keep 'em closed while you're driving.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Oh, and here's an advanced technique: if another car is coming at you, move out of the way.

jokes.text: It's just not working for non-employees.
jokes.setup: Wheeler complains the coffee machine isn't working
jokes.punchline: Louie's petty revenge disguised as mechanical problem
jokes.explanation: Good petty Louie moment. The fake mechanical explanation for his vindictiveness is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Machine's broken. I fixed it.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You want coffee? Drop a quarter in the slot like everybody else.

jokes.text: 3:00 yesterday.
jokes.setup: Louie tells Bob about a movie audition meeting time
jokes.punchline: Reveals it was yesterday, showing his deliberate sabotage
jokes.explanation: Perfectly cruel timing. The economy of '3:00 yesterday' delivers maximum impact with minimum words.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Bob, I got you a movie audition. Tomorrow at two o'clock.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Tomorrow at two? That's great, Louie!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, don't get too excited.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Why not?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Because it was yesterday at two o'clock.

jokes.text: Well, I would've given it to you yesterday, but then I would have had to cross the picket line. You know how I feel about the sanctity of picket lines.
jokes.setup: Bob realizes Louie deliberately withheld the audition message
jokes.punchline: Louie's mock principled stance while actively sabotaging the strike
jokes.explanation: Brilliant hypocrisy. Louie's fake reverence for picket lines while undermining them is perfect character writing.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? I'm running a business here. I got rules, regulations. A man's gotta have principles.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Principles? You deliberately hid that audition call!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: That's right. And I'd do it again. You know why? Because a boss without authority is like a ship without a captain.

jokes.text: While you lame-os are out there playing in the snow, I'm in here, doing a little plastic surgery on the books.
jokes.setup: Explaining how he's prepared for the hearing
jokes.punchline: Casually admits to falsifying records with euphemistic language
jokes.explanation: Good criminal confession with colorful language. 'Plastic surgery on the books' is a nice euphemism.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I'm prepared for this hearing. I've got my facts straight, my figures verified, and my records... let's just say I've creatively enhanced the documentation to reflect a more favorable accounting position.

jokes.text: Hey, Iggy. / Yo. / The Sunshine Cab Company spends more on maintenance than any other company in the city. / Our strike worked.
jokes.setup: Louie has just explained his plan to falsify records
jokes.punchline: Immediately tests it on gullible Iggy with instant success
jokes.explanation: Perfect demonstration of Louie's manipulation working. Iggy's immediate belief shows how effective the lie is.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Listen, Iggy, I need you to sign this form saying you worked a double shift yesterday.

dialogue.speaker: Iggy
dialogue.line: But I didn't work yesterday. I was home sick.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: That's what makes it perfect. Just sign here.

dialogue.speaker: Iggy
dialogue.line: Okay, boss.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: There. See? Now the records show you worked when you didn't. Beautiful.

jokes.text: All power to the people!
jokes.setup: Iggy believes the strike succeeded
jokes.punchline: Revolutionary slogan for a completely false victory
jokes.explanation: Good ironic moment. The revolutionary enthusiasm for a non-existent victory is perfectly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Iggy
dialogue.line: We did it! We won! The strike was a complete success!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Iggy, the strike failed. We got nothing.

dialogue.speaker: Iggy
dialogue.line: Nothing?! We got everything! We showed them we're united! We showed them we can't be pushed around! Today, the working man stands tall!

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Iggy, they didn't even negotiate with us.

dialogue.speaker: Iggy
dialogue.line: Negotiate? Who needs to negotiate?! We already won! From this day forward, let this be our battle cry: 'We demanded nothing, and we got it!'

jokes.text: Boy, did we eat a lot of hot dogs at that picnic, but it was for a good cause.
jokes.setup: Louie mentions a fake 'Safety First picnic' in his doctored books
jokes.punchline: Nostalgic detail about a completely fictional event
jokes.explanation: Great detail showing Louie's creative fraudulence. The fake nostalgia for a made-up event is perfectly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Safety First picnic? What Safety First picnic?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I remember that picnic. Three-legged races, horseshoes, watermelon eating contest...

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: That was a beautiful day.

jokes.text: I just hope He forgives you. / Mackenzie? He's ready to erect a statue to me. You kidding? / No, no, no, I don't mean Mackenzie.
jokes.setup: Alex's ominous reference to 'He'
jokes.punchline: Louie assumes it's his boss, missing the religious implication
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection showing Louie's worldly focus vs Alex's moral concern. Sets up the religious angle.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: He's watching us.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: The boss? When did he get here?

jokes.text: You know what that means? / Yeah. It means they got to believe you. I love this country.
jokes.setup: Alex explains about being under oath
jokes.punchline: Louie's complete misunderstanding of oath obligations plus patriotic non-sequitur
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie logic. His backwards understanding of legal obligations plus random patriotism is spot-on character work.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: When you're under oath, you have to tell the truth.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: The truth? I can't tell the truth! I'm a businessman!

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Louie, you have to tell the truth in court.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Even if it incriminates me?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Especially if it incriminates you.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: What kind of country is this? I thought this was America!

jokes.text: Yeah. It means they got to believe you. I love this country.
jokes.setup: Alex explaining what being under oath means
jokes.punchline: Louie completely misunderstands and celebrates America
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment showing Louie's complete misunderstanding of legal proceedings and twisted patriotism.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: When you're under oath, you have to tell the truth. The whole truth and nothing but the truth.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Under oath? What are you, crazy? I'm not going under anything! I'm an American!

jokes.text: Well, every great businessman has had to do that. Besides, he understands what's going on. He's a man of the world.
jokes.setup: Alex points out Louie will be lying to God
jokes.punchline: Louie's casual assumption that God is complicit in business fraud
jokes.explanation: Excellent blasphemous comedy. Louie's casual attitude toward divine judgment is perfectly outrageous and in character.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You'll be lying to God.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: So? God's in the garment business.

jokes.text: Besides, he understands what's going on. He's a man of the world.
jokes.setup: Louie will swear to God then lie
jokes.punchline: Assumes God will understand his business fraud
jokes.explanation: Brilliant blasphemous comedy - Louie treating God like a business partner who'd understand fraud. Perfect character logic.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I swear to God, I didn't cheat that guy!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: God understands — it's business.

jokes.text: Sister Porchnik.
jokes.setup: Louie mentions his Catholic school nun
jokes.punchline: The absurdly ethnic name 'Porchnik'
jokes.explanation: Mild character detail. The ethnic nun name fits Louie's background and adds texture.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Sister Mary Porchnik taught me in Catholic school.

jokes.text: It's just that thou art so easy to be holier than.
jokes.setup: Louie complains about Alex's holier-than-thou attitude
jokes.punchline: Alex's clever Biblical language response
jokes.explanation: Brilliant wordplay and perfect character moment for Alex. The Biblical language twist is expertly crafted and highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You think you're better than everybody else with your holier-than-thou attitude.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Louie, I don't think I'm better than anybody. I just try to live by certain principles.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Principles? You're killing me with principles.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Judge not lest ye be judged, Louie.

jokes.text: You and me, for four days in a mountain cabin, with no one but the furry creatures of the woods to hear our screams of ecstasy.
jokes.setup: Louie offers to meet their strike demands
jokes.punchline: Reveals his disturbing romantic fantasy as the price
jokes.explanation: Peak creepy Louie. The overly romantic language for a completely inappropriate proposition is perfectly uncomfortable.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Alright, alright, I'll meet your strike demands. But I want something in return.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I want you all to act out my fantasy. I'm a lonely man trapped in a tiny office, and you're all my beautiful secretaries who think I'm the most attractive man alive.

jokes.text: I get to bring a friend, and we sit at separate tables.
jokes.setup: Elaine trying to negotiate minimal contact on the date
jokes.punchline: Reduces the 'date' to essentially strangers dining in the same restaurant
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Elaine's desperate attempts to minimize contact. The separate tables detail is particularly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: So we're going to dinner, but we're not going to dinner together, right?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You'll go to the restaurant at 7, I'll go at 7:15.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: We'll sit at separate tables, obviously.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: We can acknowledge each other with a nod.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Maybe exchange a polite smile if the meal's going well.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: But we're essentially just two strangers who happen to be eating in the same restaurant at roughly the same time.

jokes.text: within the hearing of at least two other people, you must call me 'Stallion.'
jokes.setup: Louie counter-negotiating the date terms
jokes.punchline: His specific humiliating demand with witnesses required
jokes.explanation: Absolutely perfect Louie moment. The specific humiliation requirement with witness clause is peak creepy manipulation. Highly quotable and rewatchable.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You wanna date my daughter? Fine. But I got conditions. You gotta pick her up at 8 o'clock sharp, you bring her home by 11, and you keep your hands to yourself.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: And there's one more thing. I want three witnesses. My brother Sal, my cousin Vinny, and Father O'Brien from down at the church.

jokes.text: Except 'Stallion.' / Especially 'Stallion.'
jokes.setup: Elaine tries to negotiate away the stallion requirement
jokes.punchline: Louie's firm insistence on his most humiliating demand
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback and escalation. Louie's priorities are crystal clear - the humiliation is the whole point.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Louie, I don't understand why you need a stallion. Can't we work something out?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: No dice, Benes. You want the job? You get a stallion. That's the deal.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: But Louie, there has to be another way. What if I—

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: No. Stallion. Final answer.

jokes.text: You're taking advantage of something that's decent in me to satisfy some indecent needs of your own. How does that make you feel? / Horny as all get out.
jokes.setup: Elaine's moral outrage at Louie's manipulation
jokes.punchline: Louie's completely shameless and crude response
jokes.explanation: Absolutely brilliant dark comedy. Louie's complete shamelessness in the face of moral condemnation is perfectly executed. The contrast between Elaine's eloquent outrage and his crude response is comedy gold.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: How could you do that? That's manipulation and it's wrong!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Wrong? Kid, wrong is a luxury. I'm running a cab company here, not a charm school.

tmdb_overview: To settle the cabbies' strike, Elaine agrees to date Louie.
insight: Taxi's second season peaks at 82.6 with character work driving 44 jokes across the shutdown chaos.
